title: Plain scones
subtitle: Classic British cream tea scones
excerpt: Scones need a hot oven, cold butter, and minimal handling. Overworking the dough develops the gluten and makes them tough; the less you touch the dough, the better they rise. Cut with a sharp cutter in one straight-down press, not twisted.
sourceNotes: Scones are a British quick bread, documented from at least the early nineteenth century in Scottish and English domestic cookery. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — scones appear in the bread chapter as a quick-rise baked good leavened with baking powder. The cream tea pairing with clotted cream and jam is documented from the late Victorian period.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Warm from frozen at 180°C for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe easily. Scones are best eaten the day they are baked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cut the scones and freeze unbaked on a tray. Bake from frozen at 220°C, adding 3–4 minutes to the bake time.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of working cold butter into flour by rubbing between the fingertips until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. The cold butter breaks into tiny flakes coated in flour; when the scone bakes, these flakes melt and leave behind pockets of steam that create the lift and flaky layers. Warm hands or warm butter melts the fat too early and produces a tighter, denser crumb.

content.text: The rise in a scone comes from two things: baking powder and the
of cold butter. Work quickly so the butter stays cold, and stop mixing as soon as the dough comes together — even a minute of extra kneading flattens them.
Twist the cutter as you press down and the sides seal against themselves, blocking the rise. Press straight down and lift straight up for the tallest scones.

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Sift {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{salt-table}} into a large bowl. Rub in {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Stir in {{caster-sugar}}. Pour in nearly all the {{whole-milk}} and mix quickly to a soft, slightly sticky dough. Turn onto a floured surface and pat out to about 3 cm thick — do not roll. Stamp out rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Re-pat the trimmings and cut again.

Place on a lined baking tray, close together but not touching. Brush the tops with beaten {{eggs}} — keep the glaze off the sides or it will anchor the scone to the tray and stop it rising. Bake for 10–12 minutes until risen and deep gold on top. Eat within a few hours of baking.
